title: The Print Function
description: Master the art of displaying output to the screen.

slides.title: Mastering Print 🖨️
slides.content:
# Going Deeper with print()

You've used `print()` to show "Hello, World!" — but there's so much more it can do!

In this lesson, you'll learn:

* 🔤 Printing different types of data
* 🔗 Combining text in various ways
* ✨ Special formatting tricks

slides.title: Print Can Show Anything! 📊
slides.content:
# Not Just Text!

The `print()` function can display:

* 📝 **Text** — `print("Hello")`
* 🔢 **Numbers** — `print(42)`
* ➕ **Math results** — `print(5 + 3)`
* ✅ **True/False** — `print(True)`

Python will display whatever you put inside the parentheses!

slides.title: Print in Action! 💻
slides.content: See how print() handles different types of data:
slides.codeSnippet:
print("Hello")    # Text
print(42)          # Number
print(5 + 3)       # Math
print(True)        # Boolean

quizData.question: What will print(7 + 3) display?
quizData.explanation: Python calculates 7 + 3 first, then prints the result: 10!

slides.title: Printing Multiple Items 🔗
slides.content:
# Commas to the Rescue!

You can print multiple things at once using **commas**:

```python
print("Hello", "World")
```

Output: `Hello World`

Python automatically adds a **space** between items separated by commas!

slides.title: Multiple Items Example 💻
slides.content: Watch how commas combine different items:

quizData.question: What does print("Hi", "there") output?
quizData.explanation: Commas add a space between items — so it prints 'Hi there' (with a space)!

slides.title: String Concatenation ➕
slides.content:
# Joining Text with +

You can also combine text using the **+** operator:

```python
print("Hello" + "World")
```

Output: `HelloWorld`

**Important:** Unlike commas, the + operator does NOT add spaces automatically!

quizData.question: What's the output of print("Hello" + "Python")?
quizData.explanation: The + operator joins strings directly without adding any space!

slides.title: Comparison: Comma vs Plus 💻
slides.content: See the difference between commas and plus:
slides.codeSnippet:
# Using commas (adds space)
print("Hello", "World")

# Using plus (no space)
print("Hello" + "World")

# Adding space manually with plus
print("Hello" + " " + "World")

slides.title: Escape Characters 🔐
slides.content:
# Special Characters

**Escape characters** let you add special formatting:

* `\n` — New line (like pressing Enter)
* `\t` — Tab (adds spacing)
* `\\` — Backslash itself
* `\'` or `\"` — Quote inside quotes

They all start with a backslash `\`!

slides.title: Escape Characters in Action 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
print("Hello\nWorld")    # New line
print("Name:\tPython")    # Tab
print("She said \"Hi!\"")

quizData.question: What does \n do in a print statement?
quizData.explanation: \n is the escape character for a new line — it moves to the next line!

slides.title: The end Parameter 🛑
slides.content:
# Controlling Line Endings

By default, `print()` ends with a new line. You can change this!

```python
print("Hello", end=" ")
print("World")
```

Output: `Hello World` (on same line!)

The `end` parameter controls what comes after the printed text.

slides.title: Using end Parameter 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
print("Loading", end="")
print(".", end="")
print(".", end="")
print(".", end="")
print(" Done!")

quizData.question: What does end="" do in print()?

options.text: Prevents moving to a new line

quizData.explanation: end="" means 'end with nothing' — so the next print continues on the same line!

slides.title: The sep Parameter 🔤
slides.content:
# Custom Separators

The `sep` parameter changes what goes between items:

```python
print("a", "b", "c", sep="-")
```

Output: `a-b-c`

Default is a space, but you can use anything!

slides.title: Using sep Parameter 💻
slides.codeSnippet:
print("2024", "01", "15", sep="-")
print("red", "green", "blue", sep=" | ")
print("www", "example", "com", sep=".")

quizData.question: What's the output of print("a", "b", sep="*")?
quizData.explanation: sep="*" replaces the default space with an asterisk, giving us 'a*b'!

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content:
## Combine Everything!

You can use multiple parameters together:

```python
print("A", "B", "C", sep="-", end="!\n")
```

Output: `A-B-C!`

Mastering `print()` is your first step to creating beautiful, readable program output!
